title: Tuareg Rebels and JNIM Jihadists Seize Strategic Tessalit Military Base in Northern Mali
summary: Mali's military and Russian mercenary allies surrendered the strategic Tessalit military base near the Algerian border to a coalition of Tuareg separatists and al-Qaeda-linked JNIM jihadists, as militants called for a united front to overthrow the ruling junta.
contents:
## Fall of Tessalit Base

### Base Surrender
Forces at Mali's Tessalit military base — a strategic "super-camp" near the Algerian border — surrendered to the Tuareg-dominated FLA separatist group on May 1, with troops and their Russian mercenary allies scattering southward. A security source in Gao confirmed that "no clashes took place" during the capture, as regular troops had already evacuated when rebel forces entered.

### Strategic Significance
Tessalit functions as one of northern Mali's most important military installations, featuring a well-maintained airstrip capable of accommodating helicopters and large military aircraft. Built by French colonial forces, the base offered "a panoramic view of the entire Sahara," according to a Malian military officer. It had hosted a significant number of Malian troops, Russian mercenaries, and substantial military equipment.

### Coordinated Offensive
The fall of Tessalit follows large-scale attacks over the preceding weekend — the largest armed assault in Mali in nearly 15 years. The coordinated strikes hit key junta positions across the country, killing at least 23 people including Defense Minister Sadio Camara, who died from a car bomb at his residence in Kati, a garrison town near Bamako.

### JNIM Call to Arms
The al-Qaeda-linked Group for the Support of Islam and Muslims (JNIM) issued a statement calling for "all sincere patriots, without distinction, to rise up and unite our forces in a common front" against the junta, declaring the establishment of Sharia law as a priority for a "new Mali." JNIM simultaneously imposed a road blockade on the capital Bamako.

### Regional Implications
The crisis highlights the failure of the Russia-aligned Sahel juntas' security strategy. Mali, along with neighboring Burkina Faso and Niger, severed ties with France and turned to Russian mercenaries to combat jihadist insurgency. The three nations formed the Alliance of Sahel States (AES), claiming a joint force of 15,000 troops — now demonstrably unable to prevent major territorial losses.
